---
title: CMEMS to vertical profile
jupyter: python3
description: Convert Copernicus Marine (CMEMS) gridded data to a MIKE dfsu vertical profile.
---

This example shows how to extract a vertical transect from a Copernicus Marine (CMEMS) NetCDF file and save it as a **dfsu vertical profile** — an unstructured mesh format commonly used as boundary conditions in MIKE hydrodynamic models.

## Setup

```{python}
import numpy as np
import xarray as xr
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

import mikeio
from mikeio.spatial import GeometryFMVerticalProfile
from mikecore.DfsuFile import DfsuFileType
```

## Load CMEMS data

This example uses a small Baltic Sea subset downloaded with the [Copernicus Marine Toolbox](https://help.marine.copernicus.eu/en/collections/9080063-copernicus-marine-toolbox):

```
copernicusmarine subset \
  --dataset-id cmems_mod_bal_phy_anfc_P1D-m \
  --variable thetao --variable so \
  --minimum-longitude 23.2 --maximum-longitude 23.7 \
  --minimum-latitude 59.0 --maximum-latitude 60.0 \
  --start-datetime 2026-03-01 --end-datetime 2026-03-03 \
  --output-filename cmems_mod_bal_phy_anfc_P1D.nc
```

```{python}
ds_cmems = xr.open_dataset("../../data/cmems_mod_bal_phy_anfc_P1D.nc")
ds_cmems
```

### Regional model differences

CMEMS regional models share the same coordinate naming convention (`time`, `depth`, `latitude`, `longitude`) but differ in resolution and depth coverage.
Compare the Baltic model above with the Mediterranean model:

```{python}
ds_med = xr.open_dataset(
    "../../data/cmems_mod_med_phy-sal_anfc_4.2km_P1D.nc"
)
ds_med
```

```{python}
print(f"Baltic:        {len(ds_cmems.depth)} depth levels, max {ds_cmems.depth.values[-1]:.0f} m")
print(f"Mediterranean: {len(ds_med.depth)} depth levels, max {ds_med.depth.values[-1]:.0f} m")
```

The approach below works the same regardless of which regional model you use — just adjust the endpoints and variable names.

## Define a transect

Define a transect as evenly-spaced points between two endpoints, using the native grid resolution.

```{python}
lon_start, lat_start = 23.3, 59.3
lon_end, lat_end = 23.6, 59.6

# Use native grid spacing to determine number of points
dlat = float(ds_cmems.latitude.diff("latitude").median())
dlon = float(ds_cmems.longitude.diff("longitude").median())
dx = np.sqrt((lon_end - lon_start) ** 2 + (lat_end - lat_start) ** 2)
n_points = max(int(dx / min(dlat, dlon)), 2)

transect_lon = np.linspace(lon_start, lon_end, n_points)
transect_lat = np.linspace(lat_start, lat_end, n_points)
print(f"{n_points} points along transect (grid spacing ~{dlon:.3f}° x {dlat:.3f}°)")
```

```{python}
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ds_cmems["thetao"].isel(time=0, depth=0).plot(ax=ax, cmap="RdYlBu_r")
ax.plot(transect_lon, transect_lat, "k-o", markersize=3, label="Transect")
ax.legend()
ax.set_title("Surface temperature with transect");
```

## Extract data along the transect

Select the nearest grid point for each transect position using xarray.

```{python}
ds_transect = ds_cmems.sel(
    latitude=xr.DataArray(transect_lat, dims="points"),
    longitude=xr.DataArray(transect_lon, dims="points"),
    method="nearest",
)

depth = ds_cmems.depth.values
time = ds_cmems.time.values
n_times = len(time)
n_depths = len(depth)

temp_transect = ds_transect["thetao"].values  # (time, depth, points)
sal_transect = ds_transect["so"].values

print(f"Transect shape: {temp_transect.shape}  (time, depth, points)")
```

## Build the vertical profile mesh

Build nodes on the full CMEMS depth grid, then only create elements where data is valid.
This makes the mesh follow the bottom profile naturally.

### Nodes

```{python}
nodes_per_col = n_depths

node_x = []
node_y = []
node_z = []

for pi in range(n_points):
    for di in range(n_depths - 1, -1, -1):  # bottom to top
        node_x.append(transect_lon[pi])
        node_y.append(transect_lat[pi])
        node_z.append(-depth[di])

node_coords = np.column_stack([node_x, node_y, node_z])
print(f"Nodes: {len(node_x)}")
```

### Elements

Only create elements where all four corner nodes have valid data.
Elements below the seafloor are skipped, so the mesh follows the bathymetry.

```{python}
element_table = []
# Store corner indices for each element to map data later
elem_corners = []  # list of (di_bot, di_top, pi) tuples

# Use first variable to determine seafloor mask (all variables share the same mask)
mask = ds_transect[list(ds_transect.data_vars)[0]].values[0]  # (depth, points)

for pi in range(n_points - 1):
    for li in range(n_depths - 1):
        di_bot = n_depths - 1 - li
        di_top = n_depths - 1 - (li + 1)

        # Skip elements below the seafloor
        if any(
            np.isnan(mask[di, pi + k])
            for di in (di_bot, di_top)
            for k in (0, 1)
        ):
            continue

        bl = pi * nodes_per_col + li
        tl = pi * nodes_per_col + li + 1
        br = (pi + 1) * nodes_per_col + li
        tr = (pi + 1) * nodes_per_col + li + 1
        element_table.append(np.array([bl, br, tr, tl]))
        elem_corners.append((di_bot, di_top, pi))

n_elements = len(element_table)
print(f"Elements: {n_elements} (skipped {(n_points - 1) * (n_depths - 1) - n_elements} below seafloor)")
```

### Map data to elements

Average the four corner node values for each element. This works for any variable.

```{python}
def to_element_data(transect_data, elem_corners):
    """Map (time, depth, points) node data to (time, elements) by averaging corners."""
    n_times = transect_data.shape[0]
    out = np.empty((n_times, len(elem_corners)))
    for ei, (di_bot, di_top, pi) in enumerate(elem_corners):
        out[:, ei] = np.mean(
            [
                transect_data[:, di_bot, pi],
                transect_data[:, di_top, pi],
                transect_data[:, di_bot, pi + 1],
                transect_data[:, di_top, pi + 1],
            ],
            axis=0,
        )
    return out
```

### Geometry

```{python}
codes = np.zeros(len(node_x), dtype=int)
codes[:nodes_per_col] = 1
codes[-nodes_per_col:] = 1

geometry = GeometryFMVerticalProfile(
    node_coordinates=node_coords,
    element_table=element_table,
    codes=codes,
    projection="LONG/LAT",
    dfsu_type=DfsuFileType.DfsuVerticalProfileSigmaZ,
    n_layers=n_depths - 1,
    n_sigma=1,
)
geometry
```

## Write

```{python}
zn = np.tile(node_coords[:, 2], (n_times, 1)).astype(np.float32)

# Map each variable to elements and create DataArrays
variables = {
    "thetao": mikeio.ItemInfo("Temperature", mikeio.EUMType.Temperature),
    "so": mikeio.ItemInfo("Salinity", mikeio.EUMType.Salinity),
}

data_arrays = []
for var_name, item in variables.items():
    elem_data = to_element_data(ds_transect[var_name].values, elem_corners)
    da = mikeio.DataArray(
        data=elem_data, time=time, geometry=geometry, item=item, zn=zn,
    )
    data_arrays.append(da)

ds_dfsu = mikeio.Dataset(data_arrays)
ds_dfsu.to_dfs("transect.dfsu")
```

## Verify the result

```{python}
dfs = mikeio.open("transect.dfsu")
dfs
```

```{python}
ds_back = dfs.read()
ds_back
```

```{python}
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 4))
ds_back["Temperature"].isel(time=0).plot(ax=axes[0], cmap="RdYlBu_r")
axes[0].set_title("Temperature")
ds_back["Salinity"].isel(time=0).plot(ax=axes[1], cmap="YlGnBu")
axes[1].set_title("Salinity");
```

```{python}
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 4))
dfs.geometry.plot.mesh(ax=ax)
ax.set_title("Vertical profile mesh");
```
